rationale: The cited paper is used as a key theoretical benchmark, not just a background mention. The citing paper explicitly invokes Amaldoss and He (2010) for the static spokes-model result that prices fall with greater taste diversity when valuation is low, then contrasts this with its own BBP result where prices can rise. It also cites the 2010 paper to justify using the spokes framework for markets with diverse tastes and limited consideration sets. This comparative and model-building role is central to the citing paper's framing and contribution claims.
relation_description: The citing paper extends Amaldoss and He (2010) by adding a two-period behavior-based pricing structure to the spokes setting. It uses the 2010 static comparative statics as a baseline and shows how targeted pricing dynamics can reverse those low-valuation price effects.
